item.title: BOJ Dissent by Takaichi’s Pick Highlights Case for Faster Hikes
item.description: A single dissenting vote against the Bank of Japan’s latest interest rate hike shows the time crunch that hawks are under to get rates to a more normal level before it’s too late.

item.title: Taiwanese Brothers Amass $1 Billion From Boom in Display Chips
item.description: Biing-seng Wu has spent most of his career developing tiny chips that tell pixels on flat-screen displays how and when to light up.

item.title: US Futures Slip, Oil Climbs on Renewed Iran Threat: Markets Wrap
item.description: US stock futures fell and oil climbed as talks between Washington and Tehran were clouded by a renewed threat from President Donald Trump to strike Iran. The pound slipped on speculation around Keir Starmer’s future as UK prime minister.

item.title: Oil Climbs After Fresh Trump Threat as US-Iran Peace Talks Begin
item.description: Oil rose after President Donald Trump threatened strikes on Iran if Hezbollah keeps attacking Israel, raising concerns about progress for peace talks between Washington and Tehran.

item.title: Singapore Dollar Set to Gain Despite Hawkish Fed, Analysts Say
item.description: The Singapore dollar is poised to strengthen against the US dollar in the second half of the year despite a hawkish Federal Reserve boosting sentiment toward the greenback, according to strategists.

item.title: Trump-Backed Lawyer Wins Colombian Election in Preliminary Count
item.description: Conservative lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella narrowly won the preliminary vote count in Colombia’s presidential election, likely sweeping aside Gustavo Petro’s leftist movement and realigning Bogotá with the US.

item.title: 【今朝の5本】仕事を始める前に読んでおきたい厳選ニュース
item.description: 米イラン和平協議、ホルムズ原油輸送続く、英首相が近く退任表明か、重要鉱物、日産取締役案に不支持

item.title: Renault Pushback on Nissan Board Picks Targets Creditor Mizuho
item.description: Renault SA’s push against two of Nissan Motor Co.’s appointments to the board effectively pits the Japanese carmaker’s top shareholder against its biggest creditor.

item.title: CRH Near Biggest-Ever Deal For Arcosa, Financial Times Says
item.description: Building materials group CRH Plc. is close to its biggest deal ever to acquire competitor Arcosa Inc., and an agreement could come as soon as next week, the Financial Times reports, citing people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Bond Traders Burned by Fed’s Pivot Look to Prices Gauge, Oil
item.description: Bond traders, recently forced to reposition for the possibility of higher interest rates ahead, are looking to this week’s personal spending data for an early read on whether the market’s newly hawkish stance is warranted.

item.title: Germany, KNDS Family Owners Agree on Stake Sale Ahead of IPO
item.description: Families that own half of tankmaker KNDS NV agreed to sell a 40% stake to the German government, according to people familiar with the matter, paving the way for one of Europe’s key defense companies to go public.

item.title: Thousands Protest Czech Plan to Overhaul Public Media Funding
item.description: Thousands protested in Prague against the government’s plan to overhaul public media funding that critics say will reduce their independence and budgets.

item.title: Hungary Names OTP’s Tardos as Debt Chief in Charge of Cost Cuts
item.description: Hungary appointed Gergely Tardos, head of the research at OTP Bank Nyrt., as chief executive of the government’s Debt Management Agency, Finance Minister Andras Karman said Sunday.

item.title: A $2.8 Trillion Asset Management Star Is a Deal Away From Losing Its Crown
item.description: Amundi became Europe’s largest fund firm with the help of acquisitions. But after years without a major takeover, rivals are catching up.

item.title: Apple’s New CEO Must Rebuild a Design Team That Lost Its Way
item.description: Also: The 2027 iPhone and AirPods plans.

item.title: Guinea’s President Doumbouya Announces Ban on Raw Gold Exports
item.description: Guinean President Mamadi Doumbouya announced a ban on raw gold exports, in an effort to boost local processing of the metal and help the domestic economy.

item.title: Oil Glut Bets Are Back in Play as Crude Sinks After US-Iran Deal
item.description: A slew of all-but-forgotten niche option positions betting on an oil glut are coming back into play as crude futures slump following a peace deal between the US and Iran.

item.title: Soaring Profits in EM Build the Case for a Raging Bull Market
item.description: For the first time in four years, companies in emerging markets are beating profit estimates, giving investors a fresh reason to believe the bull market is just getting started.

item.title: The ‘Mass Affluent’ Are Losing Their Allure for Wealth Managers Navigating AI
item.description: Wealth managers keen to stay relevant in the age of artificial intelligence may soon find that clients with a mere $1 million in liquid assets are no longer worth spending human hours on.

item.title: Aluminum’s War Shock Blunted by Dark Transits and Chinese Supply
item.description: The Iran war caused one of the biggest supply shocks to ever hit the aluminum market, but the runaway price surge that many were bracing for has been blunted by the ingenuity of producers from the Middle East to China.

item.title: Three Indian Tankers Reemerge, Pointing to Hormuz Traffic Uptick
item.description: Three fully laden India-linked supertankers have re-emerged in the Gulf of Oman, adding to increased observed bi-directional traffic across the northern and southern routes of the Strait of Hormuz even as conflicting narratives over the status of transits persist.

item.title: India Seeks US Tariff Edge Over Rivals Before Enacting Trade Pact
item.description: India is seeking a competitive advantage over rival nations before rolling out a much-delayed trade agreement with the US, after both countries agreed on the initial framework.

item.title: Swiss Voters Set to Reject Stricter Neutrality, Early Poll Shows
item.description: Swiss voters are set to reject an initiative to strengthen neutrality, according to an early poll, a result that could support keeping up sanctions against Russia.

item.title: A Stock Trader’s Guide to Navigating a Rare ‘Super El Niño’
item.description: As concerns over the Iran war recede, stock investors are confronting another threat: climate risk, which is prompting a reassessment of bets across sectors from agriculture to insurance.

item.title: Fed’s Favorite Gauge Is Seen Showing Faster Inflation
item.description: The latest update to the Federal Reserve’s favorite inflation gauge is unlikely to challenge a growing consensus at the US central bank around the need for interest-rate hikes this year.

item.title: Millions in Brazil Get Fake Government Mobile Alert After Hack
item.description: Brazil’s Integration and Regional Development Ministry and federal police are investigating an intrusion into the civil defense alert system that sent messages containing the word “misanthropy” to the mobile phones of millions of Brazilians.

item.title: Trump’s Fears About Economy Undercuts US Leverage in Iran Talks
item.description: President Donald Trump said the prospect of global economic collapse was a big reason he signed an interim peace deal with Iran. That admission exposes a key US weakness heading into the next round of talks with Tehran.

item.title: Brazil’s Lula Holds Lead Against Bolsonaro After Bank Scandal
item.description: President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva maintained his lead in polls on the Brazilian presidential election against Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, who is weighed down by his ties to a former banker involved in the country’s biggest bank fraud scandal.

item.title: South Africa’s Kganyago Warns Inflation Expectations Are Rising
item.description: South Africa’s central bank governor said policymakers are seeing early signs of second-round inflation effects as underlying price pressures build, emphasizing the need to act.

item.title: Fed Governor Bowman Spoke at Private Dinner After Policy Meeting
item.description: Michelle Bowman, the Federal Reserve’s vice chair for supervision, spoke at a private dinner in New York hours after attending the central bank’s policy meeting, the Wall Street Journal reported, a period when officials are restricted from commenting on monetary policy.
